mission.title: Install and Configure Container Network Interface Cni- on Kubernetes
mission.description: The Container Network Interface (CNI) provides a specification and libraries for writing plugins to configure network interfaces in Linux containers. Installing CNI allows you to manage network connectivity for your containerized applications efficiently.

steps.title: Add the CNI Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official CNI Helm repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add cni https://charts.containernetworking.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install CNI using Helm
steps.description:
Install CNI in your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. Replace `<your-namespace>` with your desired namespace.
```bash
helm install cni cni/cni --namespace <your-namespace> --create-namespace --version v1.3.0
```

steps.title: Verify CNI installation
steps.description:
Check that the CNI pods are running in your cluster.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <your-namespace>
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for CNI
steps.description:
Edit the CNI deployment to set resource limits. Replace `<your-namespace>` with your namespace.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment cni --namespace <your-namespace>
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     memory: "512Mi"
#     cpu: "500m"
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for CNI
steps.description:
Create a Role and RoleBinding for CNI to ensure it has the necessary permissions. Replace `<your-namespace>` with your namespace.
```bash
kubectl create role cni-role --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods --namespace <your-namespace>
kubectl create rolebinding cni-rolebinding --role=cni-role --serviceaccount=<your-namespace>:default --namespace <your-namespace>
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of CNI will show the CNI pods running in your specified namespace. You will also have configured resource limits and RBAC permissions for the CNI deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add cni https://charts.containernetworking.io
helm repo update

kubectl edit deployment cni --namespace <your-namespace>
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     memory: "512Mi"
#     cpu: "500m"

kubectl create role cni-role --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods --namespace <your-namespace>
kubectl create rolebinding cni-rolebinding --role=cni-role --serviceaccount=<your-namespace>:default --namespace <your-namespace>

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the CNI Helm release from your Kubernetes cluster. Replace `<your-namespace>` with your namespace.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources associated with CNI.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the namespace used for CNI and verify that it has been removed. Replace `<your-namespace>` with your namespace.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current state of your CNI installation. Replace `<your-namespace>` with your namespace.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and upgrade the CNI release to the latest version. Replace `<your-namespace>` with your namespace.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that the CNI pods are running the new version in your cluster. Replace `<your-namespace>` with your namespace.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue occurs when CNI pods repeatedly crash. Check the logs for errors using the following command: 
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace <your-namespace>
``` 
Fix any configuration issues identified in the logs.

troubleshooting.title: CNI not functioning as expected
troubleshooting.description:
If network connectivity issues arise, verify the CNI configuration. Use the following command to check the status of the CNI pods: 
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <your-namespace>
``` 
Ensure all pods are running and check for any errors.

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter permission denied errors, verify the Role and RoleBinding for CNI. Check the roles with: 
```bash
kubectl get role --namespace <your-namespace>
kubectl get rolebinding --namespace <your-namespace>
``` 
Make sure the correct permissions are granted.

troubleshooting.title: Failed to connect to the Kubernetes API
troubleshooting.description:
If CNI fails to connect to the Kubernetes API, check the network policies and firewall settings. Use the following command to check the CNI pod logs for connection errors: 
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace <your-namespace>
``` 
Ensure that the CNI has the necessary network access.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
